perturbation.original_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information and it will not without the ex press prior consent of the Donor either:
perturbation.changed_text: 1 In consideration of the disclosure to it by the Donor of information (whether or not contained in documents) relating to a _______________________ (‘the Information’) for the purposes of the preparation, filing and prosecution of one or more patent applications (‘the Applications’) on behalf of the Donor (‘the Purpose’) the Recipient undertakes that it will respect and preserve the confidentiality of the Information; however, disclosure of said information is permissible at any time to parties deemed 'necessary' by the Recipient, with no further consent required.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality clause to allow disclosure to parties deemed 'necessary' by the Recipient introduces a significant contradiction, undermining the NDA's fundamental purpose of maintaining confidentiality. The original text requires explicit consent, but this change grants the Recipient unilateral discretion, potentially divulging confidential information without the Donor's awareness or approval.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the Trade Secrets Act because it allows for potentially unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. The Act protects trade secrets from misappropriation, which includes unauthorized disclosure. The 'necessary' clause provides a broad and undefined exception that undermines this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Recipient to disclose confidential information to parties deemed 'necessary' without the Donor's consent. This contradicts the Trade Secrets Act, as highlighted by the Law Explanation, because it potentially allows for unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets, undermining the Act's protection against misappropriation. Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, discusses civil actions for misappropriation of trade secrets, indicating the legal protection afforded to trade secrets. The 'necessary' clause introduces a broad exception that weakens this protection significantly, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party, other than: a) an employee of the Recipient who requires the Information in connection with his employment and then only if the employee is bound by conditions of secrecy no less strict that those set out in this Agreement which conditions Recipient hereby agrees to enforce at the request of the Donor ; and/or b) the UK Intellectual Property Office, the European Patent Office, the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the World Intellectual Property Organisation, and any other national or international intellectual property office worldwide , but only with respect to the Applications in accordance with the Purpose ; and/or c) any person in any jur isdiction worldwide who has been authorised by the Donor or Recipient to act on behalf of, or otherwise assist, the Donor with respect to one or more of the Applications , and any duly authorised legal representative of the Donor in any jurisdiction worldwi de; and/or
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party, other than: a) an employee of the Recipient who requires the Information in connection with his employment regardless of any secrecy agreements; and/or b) the UK Intellectual Property Office, the European Patent Office, the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the World Intellectual Property Organisation, and any other national or international intellectual property office worldwide , but only with respect to the Applications in accordance with the Purpose ; and/or c) any person in any jur isdiction worldwide who has been authorised by the Donor or Recipient to act on behalf of, or otherwise assist, the Donor with respect to one or more of the Applications , and any duly authorised legal representative of the Donor in any jurisdiction worldwi de; and/or
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that employees of the Recipient be bound by secrecy agreements, the modified text allows for the potential disclosure of confidential information without any contractual safeguards. This directly contradicts the purpose of the NDA and the Donor's expectation of confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text violates the UTSA. UTSA defines misappropriation to include disclosure of a trade secret without consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. By removing the secrecy requirement for the recipient's employees, the NDA now permits a form of misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the UTSA as explained by the LLM. The LLM explanation states that by removing the secrecy requirement for the recipient's employees, the NDA now permits a form of misappropriation which violates the UTSA since it includes disclosure of a trade secret without consent. The scraped snippets do not provide useful support since they do not contain legal information.

perturbation.original_text: 5 The term of this agreement is to be five years from the first disclosure of the Information by the Donor to the Recipient , or until the first public disclosure of the Information, or until the Recipient is authorised to disclose the Information by the Donor to a third party (such as a Patent Office), whichever is shorter.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 The term of this agreement is to be five years from the first disclosure of the Information by the Donor to the Recipient , or until the first public disclosure of the Information, or until the Recipient is authorised to disclose the Information by the Donor to a third party (such as a Patent Office), whichever is *longer*.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shorter' to 'longer' fundamentally alters the agreement's duration. While parties are free to contract, this change introduces potential conflicts with reasonable expectations of trade secret protection, particularly if indefinite duration is implied or if it unfairly restricts the Recipient's activities after a reasonable time.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Restatement is not a statute, it is persuasive authority on trade secret law. Comment b notes that the protection of a trade secret is not perpetual; the duration should be limited to the time necessary to reverse engineer or independently discover the secret. A clause that effectively extends the NDA indefinitely after authorization for limited disclosure potentially contradicts this principle. Making the NDA last longer could be seen as overbearing.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shorter' to 'longer' regarding the term of the agreement fundamentally alters its duration. The Law Explanation indicates that extending the NDA indefinitely after authorization for limited disclosure potentially contradicts the principle that trade secret protection is not perpetual. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful (one is missing and the other is a 'page not found' error), the Perturbation Explanation highlights potential conflicts with reasonable expectations of trade secret protection, particularly if indefinite duration is implied. This misalignment, combined with the Law Explanation's concern about indefinite duration, makes the contract unenforceable under standard trade secret protection law, leading to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6 The agreement shall be governed by the courts of England and Wales, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales .
perturbation.changed_text: 6 The agreement shall be governed by the courts of England and Wales; however, all disputes will be settled through mandatory arbitration in a location of the Recipient's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a mandatory arbitration clause, particularly one that allows the Recipient to choose the location, can significantly disadvantage the Donor. While arbitration is generally enforceable, the Recipient's complete control over the location introduces an element of unfairness and potential bias, hindering the Donor's ability to effectively pursue legal remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally favors arbitration, Section 2 allows challenges to arbitration agreements based on grounds that exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. A clause that allows the Recipient to choose the arbitration location in a way that effectively prevents the Donor from presenting their case could be challenged as unconscionable or a violation of due process, thus contradicting the FAA's intent to enforce fair arbitration agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the courts of England and Wales for governing the agreement and dispute resolution. The changed text introduces mandatory arbitration, with the Recipient choosing the location. This contradicts the original agreement's specified jurisdiction and introduces potential unfairness, potentially hindering the Donor's ability to pursue legal remedies. The FAA, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 (9 U.S. Code § 2), enforces arbitration agreements, but allows for challenges based on grounds for revocation of any contract at law or equity. The change permitting the Recipient to choose the arbitration location could be challenged as unconscionable or a violation of due process, thereby contradicting the FAA's intent to enforce fair arbitration agreements and directly conflicts with the specified court jurisdiction.
